coredata.dc:title: A novel biodegradable nanoparticle as a robust biodiesel antioxidant, and fuel additive for improvement of diesel engine performance and exhaust emission

Feyzi, Mostafa
Norouzi, Leila
coredata.dc:description: Pomegranate peel carbon quantum dots were synthesized through a hydrothermal process. This innovative green nanoparticle underwent characterization using UV–visible spectroscopy, TEM, FT-IR, XRD, CHN, and N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. The effects of pomegranate peel carbon quantum dots on greenhouse gas emissions from diesel engines and the efficiency of diesel engines were assessed by utilizing wasted frying oil biodiesel with a B20 volumetric concentration. Different concentrations of pomegranate peel CQDs (100, 300, and 600ppm) were tested for each fuel blend. The results indicated that when compared to D100 fuel, the optimal concentration of carbon quantum dots (600 ppm) led to significant improvements in torque, power, CO, UHC, and NOx levels by 12.113 %, 18.606 %, 14.12 %, 33.85 %, and 26 %, respectively. Furthermore, the oxidation stability of biodiesel was examined using the Rancimat technique, revealing that biodiesel derived from wasted frying oil exhibited low oxidation stability (6 h). To enhance the stability, three antioxidants were tested: pomegranate peel carbon quantum dot, synthetic antioxidant BHA, and alcoholic extract of pomegranate peel. Accelerated tests using the Rancimat technique demonstrated a substantial increase in the storage stability of wasted sunflower oil biodiesel when mixed with pomegranate peel carbon quantum dot (92.21 h), BHA (36.5 h), and alcoholic extract of pomegranate peel (12.48 h) at a concentration of 7000 ppm. Overall, these findings suggest that pomegranate peel carbon quantum dots serve as effective antioxidants for biodiesel and as alternative fuel additives to enhance diesel exhaust emissions and engine efficiency.

head.ce:title: A novel biodegradable nanoparticle as a robust biodiesel antioxidant, and fuel additive for improvement of diesel engine performance and exhaust emission

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, P. O. Box: +98-67149, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, P. O. Box: +98-67149, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pomegranate peel carbon quantum dots were synthesized through a hydrothermal process. This innovative green nanoparticle underwent characterization using UV–visible spectroscopy, TEM, FT-IR, XRD, CHN, and N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. The effects of pomegranate peel carbon quantum dots on greenhouse gas emissions from diesel engines and the efficiency of diesel engines were assessed by utilizing wasted frying oil biodiesel with a B20 volumetric concentration. Different concentrations of pomegranate peel CQDs (100, 300, and 600ppm) were tested for each fuel blend. The results indicated that when compared to D100 fuel, the optimal concentration of carbon quantum dots (600 ppm) led to significant improvements in torque, power, CO, UHC, and NOx levels by 12.113 %, 18.606 %, 14.12 %, 33.85 %, and 26 %, respectively. Furthermore, the oxidation stability of biodiesel was examined using the Rancimat technique, revealing that biodiesel derived from wasted frying oil exhibited low oxidation stability (6 h). To enhance the stability, three antioxidants were tested: pomegranate peel carbon quantum dot, synthetic antioxidant BHA, and alcoholic extract of pomegranate peel. Accelerated tests using the Rancimat technique demonstrated a substantial increase in the storage stability of wasted sunflower oil biodiesel when mixed with pomegranate peel carbon quantum dot (92.21 h), BHA (36.5 h), and alcoholic extract of pomegranate peel (12.48 h) at a concentration of 7000 ppm. Overall, these findings suggest that pomegranate peel carbon quantum dots serve as effective antioxidants for biodiesel and as alternative fuel additives to enhance diesel exhaust emissions and engine efficiency.

Pomegranate peel carbon quantum dot, synthetic antioxidant of BHA and alcoholic was used. The accelerated experiments utilizing the Rancimat method displayed a significant improvement in the oxidative stability of the wasted sunflower oil biodiesel doped with Pomegranate peel carbon quantum dot (92.21 h), BHA (36.5 h), and alcoholic extract of pomegranate peel (12.48 h) for samples doped with 7000 ppm.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Careful consideration of economic, social, and political factors is necessary to reduce the impacts of global climate change, with a primary focus on decreasing the use of fossil fuels. Various initiatives introduced to explore alternative sources of sustainable energy. Biodiesel has garnered considerable attention within the realm of biofuels due to its resemblance to traditional diesel in terms of chemical composition and energy content. It possesses the ability to function as a viable transportation fuel without necessitating modifications to existing diesel engines, as it is compatible with a variety of engine models. Biodiesel, characterized by its advantageous physicochemical properties such as biodegradability, non-toxicity, and low sulfur content, emerges as a promising alternative to diesel fuel. Moreover, a notable benefit of biodiesel is its elevated flash point of 423 K, rendering it non-flammable and non-explosive. With a high cetane number, reduced CO emissions, and superior lubrication, biodiesel offers safe storage, handling, and transportation options with minimal associated risks compared to conventional fossil fuels [1,2]. Biodiesel can be produced through transesterification reactions using different sources such as edible oils, inedible oils, animal fats, waste oils, and single cell oils. In this process, triglycerides converted into glycerol and alkyl esters with the help of a catalyst [3]. Research has demonstrated that pure biodiesel can be used efficiently in diesel engines without requiring major modifications to the existing hardware. This renewable fuel can be employed in compression ignition engines either independently or mixed with traditional diesel fuel in specific proportions [4]. The combustion process in these diesel engines is carried out using hot compressed air [5]. The major drawback of compression-ignition engine is the release of unwanted polluting gases throughout fuel combustion. In addition, it has characteristics for instance high compression ratio, low fuel consumption, low pump output, high thermal efficiency, and high-energy consumption [6]. Biodiesel has a high oxygen concentration, which improves fuel quality and combustion, and cleans exhaust fumes [7]. Compared to diesel fuel, lower energy value, poor cold flow property, high nitrogen oxide (NOx) emission, can be mentioned as adverse properties of biodiesel [8,9]. In addition, utilization of pure biodiesel high percentage of biodiesel in CI engines may lead to incomplete combustion and carbon sediment. Combination of alcohol, e.g. ethanol and methanol, reported as an effective approach to increase biodiesel combustion efficiency. However, utilization of alcoholic components as the primary additive in biodiesel can cause low thermal value, low stability, low cetane number, low lubricating ability and high auto ignition temperature [10,11]. Reports suggest that incorporating nanoparticle additives is expected to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and improve combustion efficiency in biodiesel. The potential application of several chemicals (non-metallic [12,13], originated antioxidant [14,15], water emulsion fuels [16], metal-based [17–21], organic [22], carbon nanotubes [22,23]) as fuel additive have been studied in recent years.

Nanoparticles derived from different metal oxides, such as Al2O3, CeO2, MgO, TiO2, CuO, ZrO2, and ZnO, are frequently employed as additives in fuel to improve the efficiency of combustion. These metal oxide nanoparticles, when introduced into the fuel during the combustion process, provide additional oxygen and effectively reduce the release of harmful exhaust gases. Research studies have indicated that the remarkable reactivity of these nanoparticles leads to early combustion and a decrease in the time it takes for ignition to occur. As a result, this enhanced combustion behavior leads to an elevation in Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE) and a reduction in Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC). On the other hand, various carbonaceous fuel additives, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), graphene oxide, and graphene nanoplates, have been integrated into diesel or biodiesel blended fuel to enhance performance and minimize emissions [11,12]. Regrettably, metal nanoparticle fuel additives have the potential to persist in the environment for an extended duration, thereby heightening worries regarding human health [24]. In response to this challenge, current research efforts have shifted towards carbon-based nanoparticles. Carbon-based nanomaterials offer a more eco-friendly alternative to metal-based fuel additives due to their lack of metallic components. The incorporation of nano carbon material into biodiesel has the potential to enhance its combustion efficiency and minimize emissions through the facilitation of uniform heat transfer and the augmentation of the reaction surface area. Moreover, the utilization of nano carbon material as a fuel additive can effectively diminish the viscosity and density of biodiesel, thereby facilitating its smooth flow and injection into engines [19]. Besides, compared to metal-based nanoparticles, carbon-based one's result in lower diesel emissions and lower engine fuel consumption that is owing to chemical properties and their superior thermal [25]. Biodiesel usage is restricted due to their instability and sedimentation in fuel. Extensive research performed to improve biodiesel properties nanocarbon contents to address these issues. Quantum dots, a novel category of carbon-based nanomaterials, have been unearthed and extensively examined in numerous scientific investigations [15,25]. These nanoparticles are very small nanocrystals (2–10 nm), that accidently explored by Xu et al. [26]. Furthermore, these carbon materials have fluorescent properties, and benefits such as low toxicity, high economic value, and uncomplicated preparation method. Quantum dots can be produced from biomaterial such as citrate (citrate acid salt), ethanol, orange juice, strawberry juice, fruit peel, watermelon peel, sugarcane, eggs, gelatin, and many others [27–31]. A blend of diesel and biodiesel carbon quantum dots has been utilized to optimize fuel combustion, improve CI engine efficiency and decrease pollutant emissions [4]. Biodiesel is a fuel formed from monoalkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids. This fatty acid chain is a mixture of both saturates and unsaturated fatty acids. Biodiesel's unsaturated fatty acid content increases its oxidation stability. Unsaturated fatty acid content and oxidation susceptibility in biodiesel have a positive correlation [32]. In general, the oxidation of biodiesel has three stages: initiation, propagation, and termination. During the initiation process, pro-oxidants agents rush double bonds in the ester carbon chain, remove hydrogen atoms, and generate carbon-centered radicals (R•). The formation of proxy radicals (ROO•) triggers the propagation step. At the termination stage, all radicals react with each other, resulting in stable products. Various factors, including air, light, metals, solids, water, and high temperatures, contribute to this problem [33]. Changes in the biodiesel sample's physicochemical properties are caused by these products, which include aldehydes, acids, ketones, and polymers. These components have the capacity to cause engine breakdown if left unchecked [34]. Therefore, retarding the oxidation of fuel is important for biodiesel engine applications. Antioxidants contribute a hydrogen atom or an electron to the free radicals, transforming them into more stable and non-radical entities. This act of donation effectively counteracts the free radicals, inhibiting their interaction with other molecules and consequently interrupting the progression of oxidation [35]. Synthetic phenolic compounds such as tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ), pyrogallol (PY), butylated hydroxyanisole (BHA), propyl gallate (PG), butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT), and are presently the most frequently utilized antioxidants [36]. Although, commercial antioxidants are high-priced, and some of them are toxic or carcinogenic. Therefore, this facet of biodiesel application has to be improved, not only to save money and increase biodiesel oxidation stability all through storage, but also to lessen ecological effect not only to save money and increase stability of biodiesel all through storage, but also to decrease ecological impact. Thus, interest in natural antioxidants has grown significantly over the past decade [37]. Several studies are evaluated varied naturalistic antioxidants as various sources to inhibit biodiesel deterioration. Extracts of potato, walnut shell, ginger, and the other plants [38–40], some bio-oil have been used as antioxidants for biodiesel and showed effects resemble the synthetic antioxidants [41]. Pomegranate juice, peel, and seed contain a significant amount of phenolic content, which has been related to the fruit's antioxidant activity [42].

The research utilized pomegranate peel as a biodegradable green precursor. Pomegranates are plentiful in the western region of Iran. It is well-established that pomegranate peel possesses antioxidant characteristics. Furthermore, both antioxidants and carbon quantum dots exhibited promising results in diesel engine applications. Subsequently, this eco-friendly precursor was employed in the production of carbon quantum dots. Herein, this highly active nanoparticle was employed in two different examinations. To the best of our knowledge, the oxidation stability of biodiesel was increased by adding carbon quantum dot. In addition, the potential of green carbon quantum dot obtained from alcoholic extract of pomegranate peel was studied for improvement of performances of CI engine and reduction of exhaust pollutant emissions (NOX, CO2, CO, and UHC). The evaluation results illustrated a remarkable potential of synthesized carbon quantum dots to improve performances of CI engine and decrease exhaust pollutant emissions.

All the materials utilized during this research have been of high purity, following sources: Ca (NO3) 2.4 H2O, Zn (NO3)2.6H2O, Fe (III) nitrate nanohydrate (Fe (NO3)3.9H2O, 25 % aqueous of NH3, Ethanol (96 %), NaOH were purchased from Merck Company, Pomegranate was purchased from domestic market.

Alcoholic extracts from pomegranate peels were utilized to synthesize carbon dots via a hydrothermal approach. Initially, the peels were dried in an oven at temperatures ranging from 30 to 40 °C. Following this, the dried peels were ground using a grinder and stored at ambient temperature until the extraction phase commenced. A total of 2 g of the finely ground material was subjected to extraction with 20 milliliters of 96% ethanol at room temperature (25 °C) for a duration of 48 hours. Subsequently, the extract underwent filtration, and the resulting solution was placed in a 30-milliliter autoclave, where it was subjected to a temperature of 200 °C for 2 h. After this process, the mixture was allowed to cool to 25 °C, and the remaining solids were separated through filtration, mirroring the procedure employed in the earlier step. The carbon quantum dots were characterized by instruments listed below.

A UV spectrometer, Agilent 8453 utilized to investigate the UV–visible absorption spectra. The TEM (Philips CM120) monitoring recorded the CQDs size and shape. XRD diffractometer (Philips X'Pert Pro) used to evaluate the chemical state, composition, and crystal structure. Utilizing an Autosorb analyzer, the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms are employed to evaluate the distribution of pore sizes and the specific surface area (Quanta chrome, Autosorb-1-mp). In addition, the Bruker FTIR instrument utilized to display the functional groups on the catalyst's surface. The FTIR spectra of CQDs obtained using (Thermo FTIR instrument Avatar model). CHN (LECO -TruSpec model) (LECO -TruSpec model).

The biodiesel utilized in this research was produced in previous research [43]. Physicochemical properties of biodiesel listed in Table 1 . Density is a fundamental physical characteristic that is employed to determine the precise volume of fuel needed for efficient combustion. Consequently, the density of biodiesel has a significant impact on engine performance.

Additionally, the density of the fuel plays a role in the breakup of the fuel spray from the injector [44,45]. The density of biodiesel was measured using a density meter Anton Paar DMA4500 and found to be 874 kg/m3, which falls within the range of standard fuel density. Another important property of biodiesel's physical chemistry is its kinematic viscosity, which allows for the atomization of the fuel. Moreover, in a fuel delivery system, the viscosity of the fuel affects the lubrication of the system. An unfavorable viscosity, whether too low or too high, can hinder the proper functioning of the engine. If the viscosity is too low, the fuel fails to adequately lubricate the injection system, leading to accelerated fatigue and leakage issues. Conversely, if the viscosity is too high, excessive energy is required to spray the fuel, preventing stable dispersion and resulting in the formation of suspended particles and exhaust gases. As biodiesel is a blend of various fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs), each component contributes to the overall viscosity. Therefore, it may be possible to establish a relationship between viscosity and the content of fatty acid methyl esters in biodiesel [46,47]. The kinematic viscosity of biodiesel was measured using a Ubbelohde Viscometer and found to be 3.52 mm2s-1 at 40 °C, which falls within the acceptable viscosity range for biodiesel according to the ASTM standard D6751. The refractive index (RI) is an optical property that can be used to assess the degree of ester saturation in biodiesel. In fact, the RI is influenced by factors such as molecular weight, fatty acid chain length, degree of unsaturation, and degree of conjugation [48]. In addition, oxidation stability of biodiesel was obtained by Rancimat instrument. The experiments were carried out utilizing a Rancimat instrument (Rancimat 743, Metrohm, Ltd., Herisau, Switzerland) based on the EN 14214 standard. The biodiesel shows 6 h IP in Rancimat examination. According to the European Standard EN 14214, the minimum oxidation stability requirement for biodiesel is 6 h at 110 °C. The calorific value (CV) of biodiesel, often referred to as the heating value, serves as a critical metric for assessing the energy released during its combustion. This value can exhibit minor variations depending on the feedstock utilized, such as soybean oil, rapeseed oil, or waste cooking oil; however, it typically resides within a defined range. Although standards such as EN 14214 and ASTM D6751 do not establish a minimum CV requirement, it is crucial for biodiesel to align with the specifications pertinent to its intended use. For instance, biodiesel derived from waste frying oil generally presents calorific values between 37 and 41 MJ/kg. The CV of biodiesel was determined using the ASTM D240 Bomb Calorimeter, specifically the BAXI model BXT-ZDHW-9L, yielding a measurement of 39.5 MJ/kg [49]. The cetane rating, also referred to as the cetane number, serves as an indicator of the ignition quality of diesel fuel. An elevated cetane number signifies a more rapid ignition process. Generally, the cetane number tends to increase with greater saturation and longer carbon chain lengths, while it diminishes with shorter chain lengths and higher degrees of unsaturation and branching. This phenomenon explains why alkanes typically exhibit higher cetane numbers compared to alkenes and aromatic compounds. The established methods for assessing the cetane number of biodiesel include the ASTM D613 and ISO 5165 testing protocols, which utilize a monocylindrical cetane engine. According to ASTM D6751, biodiesel must achieve a cetane number of at least 47, with minimal deviation from conventional standard methods [50]. The cetane number of biodiesel was assessed 52 using the Zeltex model ZX-101XL. The flash point serves as a vital safety metric, representing the minimum temperature at which a liquid emits sufficient vapor to ignite upon contact with a flame. This parameter is essential for preventing the contamination of highly volatile substances [51]. In the present study, the flash point of waste sunflower biodiesel was determined to be 160 °C, as indicated in Table 1, utilizing the standard ASTM D93 with the Digital Pensky Martin Close Cup Flash Point Tester from Stanhope_SETA. According to ASTM D6751, biodiesel must possess a flash point exceeding 93 °C (200 °F) to guarantee safe storage and handling. Similarly, the EN 14214 standard mandates a minimum flash point of 120 °C (248 °F). Therefore, the waste sunflower biodiesel produced in this study is seem safe.

A 3.0 g biodiesel was tested in a reaction chamber warmed to 110 °C with a 20 L/min infusion of pure air. In the beginning, the measurement vessel is filled with deionized water with a conductivity of less than 5.0 S/cm. Water conductivity gradually increases during the analysis. When volatile compounds dissociate, conductivity abruptly increases, indicating the time of biodiesel oxidation, known as the induction period (IP).

A series of tests were conducted to assess the key parameters of the engine, including torque, specific fuel consumption, and power, in order to analyze pollutant emissions such as unburned hydrocarbons (UHC), nitrogen oxides (NOx), carbon monoxide (CO), and carbon dioxide (CO2). For this analysis, a single-cylinder diesel engine was employed in conjunction with an eddy current test bench. The configuration of the diesel engine and dynamometer is presented in Fig. 1 , while the specifications of the single-cylinder engine are detailed in Table 2 . A centralized monitoring system was integrated into both the engine and dynamometer to track and log the relevant parameters. To assess the greenhouse gas emissions from the exhaust, the AVL DITEST GAS 1000 pollution analyzer, manufactured by AVL in Germany was utilized. This device is capable of accurately detecting and quantifying various gases, including CO, CO2, UHC, and NOx. The precision and detection range for all pollutants were evaluated using the analyzer, with the findings documented in Table 3 . It is important to highlight that all tests were conducted under full load conditions at three engine speeds, specifically between 1800 and 2400 rpm, at atmospheric pressure and a room temperature of 30 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of pomegranates carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are primarily amorphous fluorescent nanoparticles composed of a sp2/sp3 carbon network with sizes ranging from 10 to 15 nm. The predominant elements in them are carbon, oxygen, hydrogen, and nitrogen, and the usage of precursors and reaction circumstances has a significant impact on the composition as a whole. CQDs are interesting because of their easy and scalable synthesis from cheap and plentiful precursors like amino acids and carbohydrates [6].

TEM revealed the size and morphology of the CQDs. Fig. 5 depicts the size range of CQDs from two to 10 nm. The percent of carbon, hydrogen and nitrogen of carbon quantum dot are analyzed by CHN depicted in Table 4 . Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were utilized to measure the pore volume distribution, average of pore width, specific surface area, and 0/81 cm3 g-1 , 17A (at77K), and 474.64 m2 g−1 (Fig. 6 ). The IUPAC classification BET analysis revealed type IV as the physio absorption isotherms of the prepared CQDs. The correlation between the hysteresis loop's form and the catalyst's texture and microstructure was discovered [28]. Furthermore, BET analysis demonstrated the mesoporous structure of CQDs. Mesoporous pores with a sizable surface area were present.

Maintaining and checking the quality of biodiesel all through storage is an importance for fuel producers, users, and suppliers. In this regard, the availability of quick tests for determining the degree of degradation represents an important step towards improving biodiesel managing and trading. Fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopes are previously widely used in the field of biofuel characterization and analysis. Recently, it was discovered that the change in dielectric property is consistent with the Rancimat conductivity during the degradation process [55].

UV–vis spectroscopy can detect conjugated unsaturation, aromatic groups, and conjugated systems with nonbinding electrons. Thus, UV–vis spectroscopy can detect the formation of biodiesel oxidation products such as dienes (primary oxidation), trienes, and other by-products such as ketones and aldehydes [36]. The UV–vis absorption spectra of biodiesel before and after degradation depicted in Fig. 7 . Absorption bands with maxima round the 232, 275 were observed for both samples. Compounds with two conjugated C=C double bonds have an absorption band around 232 nm (conjugated dienes, e.g. conjugated linoleic acid). Conjugate trienes (i.e. eleostearic acid) are also responsible for absorption in the 260–290 nm range [51]. The spectroscopic analysis of fluorescence could be a useful tool in studies involving physics: chemical options for biodiesel [56]. Fluorescence spectroscopy of biodiesel sample before and after degradation was shown in Fig. 8 . The fluorescence intensity at round 520 nm elevated. This fluorescence band is partly attributed to oxidized products generated in the course of the degradation technique, (e.g. conjugated tetraenes [57,58].

Oxidized products can be quantified either directly via fluorescence method or indirectly via Rancimat method (by increasing the conductivity). Fluorescence method has higher sensitivity, accuracy, and portability compared to Rancimat method. However, additional testing is required to evaluate the performance of the fluorescence approach with biodiesel achieved from various sources [59,60].

The Rancimat induction period is explained as the time it takes for the volatile acid production rate to reach the inflection point. In the Rancimat instrument, biodiesel is first oxidized, producing hydroperoxides, and then secondary oxidized, producing volatile components such as alcohol, carbonyl compounds, and olefins. Non-volatile by-products are further broken down and serve as additional sources of volatile compounds. Due to the auto-oxidation that occurs in before the biodiesel is introduced into the Rancimat equipment, volatile acids are present in different types of biodiesel storage. However, fresh fuels have significantly low levels of volatile acids. Volatile acids speed up the production of volatile acids when they are present in the device from the early stages. As a result, this event is characterized by a simultaneous increase in storage time and decrease in Rancimat induction time [59]. The Rancimat test examined three times. The average of them reported as Induction time. The Rancimat induction time of waste cooking oil biodiesel with pomegranate peel alcoholic extract, BHA, and pomegranate peel carbon quantum dots was shown in Table 5 . The three different sample were added with the same concentration (7000 ppm).

A standard deviation close to zero indicates that data points are very close to the mean, whereas a larger standard deviation indicates data points are spread further away from the mean. The standard deviation, which quantifies how near the data are to the estimated mean, may be used to judge whether an experiment is exact or not. As a result, standard deviation and accuracy are inversely proportional: the higher the standard deviation, the less exact the experiment. The results of the standard deviation showed that the data dispersion of the test was low, which indicates the high accuracy of the test [61]. The neat biodiesel shows 6 h IP in Rancimat examination. The BHA IP was estimated 36.15 h. Butylated hydroxyanisole (BHA) would be a white, waxy solid, which is oil soluble but water insoluble. It is slightly more effective than BHT [62]. It has previously reported that BHA could improve storage stability of biodiesel. The oxidation time of soybean oil from 0.16 h increased to 8.18 h by adding BHA at 1500 ppm, shows evidence of its possible scavenger behavior. In addition, the use of BHA at 2000 ppm enhanced the time required for castor oil biodiesel to oxidize from 6 to 15 h [38]. As a result, the added BHA with concentration of 7000 ppm was in accordance with the reports. The ethanol extract of pomegranate peel and pomegranate peel carbon quantum dots show 12.15, 92.21 h IP in the stability oxidation of biodiesel respectively. Sagaste et al. [63] Storage stability of canola-based biodiesel investigated by adding extract of Larrea tridentate. This test was done in Rancimat instrument at 110 °C and discovered that adding 1000 mg/L antioxidant in the neat biodiesel oxidation time was improved. The oxidation time of biodiesel increased from 1.25 to 32.27 h, respectively. Furthermore, França et al. [64] investigated the effect of Moringa oleifera Lam leaf extracts on oxidation time of a commercial biodiesel. The time of oxidation of pure biodiesel was estimated 5.51 h. When 4 concentrations of Moringa ethanoic extract were added to biodiesel (1000–4000 ppm) biodiesel IP were increased 7.75, 8.67, 9.02, and 9.61 h, respectively. Khounani et al. [65] and Ahanchi et al. [39] can increase the oxidation time of wasted vegetable oil, and canola biodiesel by adding walnut husk extract (WHE) and pistachio hull extract (PHE) in concentrations of 4000 and 2500 ppm respectively. C-dots can be engineered to function as both generators of reactive oxygen species and as radical scavengers. However, the effectiveness of C-dots in their role as radical scavengers is not uniform and is highly contingent upon the specific type of radical involved. This variability arises from the differing mechanisms of action, which necessitate that the materials be tailored for particular functions to achieve optimal performance. Key properties such as proton or electron donor capabilities are critical, alongside the relative proportions of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon sites within the C-dots. The ability to donate protons is linked to the presence of various surface functional groups, including -OH, -COOH, and -NH2. However, this is not the sole requirement for scavenging reactive nitrogen species; the extent of sp2 sites, which contribute to the stabilization of electronic charge and the formation of adducts, is also crucial. In the context of hydroxyl species, the electron donor capability becomes essential, particularly when large sp2 domains are present. While doping with electron donor atoms presents a viable strategy, it is important to recognize that antioxidant activity results from a combination of factors. Therefore, distinguishing clearly between GQSs, CQDs, CNDs, and PCDs is essential for the effective design of antioxidant materials [66]. The mechanisms underlying radical scavenging can operate independently or synergy, including:
1.
Hydrogen transfer from surface functional groups.
2.
Electron transfer mechanism (ETM), which can be illustrated using a donor-acceptor map (DAM) that assesses the electron donation or acceptance potential of a molecule.
3.
The formation of radical adducts at sp2 carbon sites, where sp2-rich domains facilitate the interaction with radical species, leading to electron delocalization in conjugated graphene-like regions and the subsequent formation of additional adducts [67].

Antioxidants are categorized into enzymatic types, such as superoxide dismutase, catalase, peroxidase, and ascorbate peroxidase, as well as nonenzymatic types, which include polyphenols, phenolic acids, flavonoids, and ascorbic acid. Carbon dots are classified among the nonenzymatic radical scavengers. Their radical scavenging activity exhibits significant variability influenced by their composition and structure, as well as the specific types of radicals involved. Consequently, the discussion of radical scavenging activity is tailored to reflect the response of different radicals and the unique characteristics of various classes of carbon dots [68].

The engine's performance and emissions are influenced by the fuel characteristics, engine design, and operating parameters. Different types of fuel yield varying engine outputs when used in the same engine. In comparison to diesel, biodiesel has a lower heating value, higher density, higher viscosity, and increased oxygen content. These factors play a crucial role in determining the engine's performance. Biodiesel can only be used in diesel engines in limited blend proportions due to its differing properties from diesel [69]. The impact of different concentration of pomegranate peel CQDs (100,300, and 600 ppm) on performance, and emission parameters of a diesel engine was evaluated using waste cooking oil methyl ester B20 mixture.

Torque, Power, BSFC and BTE were estimated. Then compared with the one from baseline fuels (i.e., neat diesel, B20). The emission parameters were also measured and their results were reported in Figs. 9–12
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Power and torque, BSFC and BTE
ce:section.ce:para: The engine torque at different engine speeds was measured for different fuel blends (Fig. 9). The results display that raising the engine speed from 1800 to 2400 rpm, diminished engine torque. Due to incompleteness of cylinder filling at high speeds, the compression pressure decreased, which in result reduced the engine torque. Fuel combustion pressure is related to fuel thermal value [11]. Compared to diesel, the energy liberalization process of pure biodiesel fuel had higher delay, which could be related to its higher carbon concentration [11]. The low thermal value of biodiesel leads to a reduction of mean effective pressure. This reduces the torque drops and the force on the piston surface. As a result, utilizing neat biodiesel instead of neat diesel reduce the engine's torque. In addition, the addition of biodiesel to diesel fuel (between 5 % and 20 %) improve combustion, releasing more energy and raising average pressure in the fuel tank. This enhancement in the torque and power generated. In this study, yield of all fuel sample compared with the D100 were estimated in Table 5. B20 boosted the torque with the yield of 4.52 %. In addition, as it shown in Fig. 9, CQDs blend with B20 increased the torque. The highest torque with the yield of 12.11 % was observed at 2400 rpm by adding 600 ppm CQDs to the blend of B20. By utilizing nanoparticles (NPs), fuel can be altered to enhance its efficiency [70].

The unique properties of CQDs, such as small size 3–5 nm and high surface area, caused to excelling dispersion in the fuel. Therefore, combustion carry out more completely [11]. Fig. 10 displays the impact of adding CQDs in three concentration of 100, 300, 600 ppm to B20 at three speed rates of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm on the engine power. The maximum level of power at the 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm belong to B20 + CQDs 600 ppm. The power for B20 + CQDs 100 ppm sample was increased by 14.64 %, 18.39 % and 16.08 % compared with the D100 respectively. Due to CQDs has more oxygen in its components, engine combustion improved and, as a result, engine power increased. This improvement could be generated by increasing the proportion of the surface-to-volume of CQDs and the heat transmission coefficient ascribed. According to some studies, adding nanoparticles to fuel improves power and torque. As a result, adding nanoparticles improves the mixing of air and fuel. As a result, improved combustion chamber oxygenation and combustion that is more complete, torque and power are increased. Similar results were reported in other experiments [6,18,and 71]].

BSFC is known to as the fuel consumption to power ratio. One of the most important metrics for examining and analyzing how different fuels affect is this measurement [23]. It is the result of the energy conversion performance of intrinsically collaborated combustion [72]. The effect of adding CQDs on Break specific fuel consumption (BSFC) was also investigated in Fig. 11
. The best result of this parameter of performance of engine is related to B20 + CQDs 600 ppm at speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm. Diminish amount of BSFC for the B20 + CQDs 600 ppm sample were observed 14.12 %, 12.94 % and 13.33 % compared to D100 at the speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 11 by increasing the concentration of nanoparticle the fuel consumption decreased. Because the reduction in BSFC may be associated to the addition of nanoparticles to B20, resulting in increased heat release, reduced ignition delay time leading to perfectly combustion, and reduced viscosity leading to higher motor performance [11].

Brake thermal efficiency (BTE) serves as a vital performance indicator for diesel engines, quantifying the ratio of mechanical work output to the energy input derived from fuel combustion. This metric reflects the engine's capability to transform the chemical energy contained in fuel into effective mechanical work. The BTE % formula, delineated in equation (1). Represented by Ferguson [73], illustrates the impact of various fuels and blends on engine performance, representing the ratio of the actual brake work produced by the engine to the chemical energy supplied by a specific fuel. Fig. 12 illustrates the thermal efficiency across three distinct engine speeds for all fuels tested. The BTE of the diesel engine exhibits an inverse relationship with both brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC) and the heating value of the fuel. The highest BTE was recorded for B20+C600 ppm. Notably, biodiesel fuels, both with and without additives, demonstrate superior BTE compared to conventional diesel fuel. This enhancement can be attributed to the relatively high surface-area-to-volume ratio of the nanoparticles involved, which serve as effective catalysts. The incorporation of these nanoparticles into the fuel facilitates improved mixing of air and fuel, thereby enhancing the chemical reactivity of the combustion process, leading to superior engine performance and reduced emissions. The elevated oxygen content present biodiesel and carbon nanoparticles contributes to the combustion reaction, thereby augmenting combustion efficiency and resulting in increased BTE. This higher oxygen content is associated with the abundance of oxygen functional groups on the surfaces of the nanoparticles, as indicated by investigations into carbon quantum dots nanostructures [20]. The most significant increase in BTE relative to diesel fuel was observed for 16.43 % at 1800 rpm speed.

The impact of the examined fuels on the exhaust gas temperatures (EGTs) of the examined engine depicted in Fig. 13 . Engine exhaust temperature was one of the critical parameters that used to indicate cylinder combustion temperature. This is a useful parameter for examining tailpipe emissions, particularly NOx in the outcomes of the tests [74]. The result of diesel operation in EGT was between 1 % and 7 % lower than other fuels (on average in the speed range). A longer ignition postponement resulted in postponed combustion and a higher EGT. Though biodiesel and its mixture have a higher carbon number, their poor atomization (resulting from their higher viscosity than diesel) increased the existence of unburned fuels in the premixed phase of combustion. The diffusion burn phase will eventually cause these unburned components to burn, raising the temperature of the exhaust gas [75].

CO2, CO, NOx, sulfuric contaminants, and unburned hydrocarbons (UHCs) are all common components of fossil fuel emissions [12,16]. Complete combustion processes and sufficient oxygen availability cause CO2 emission [12]. Some researchers reported that, owing to the low carbon/hydrogen ratio of biodiesel during the complete combustion. These fuels yield lower CO2 emissions compared to diesel [20]. In general, compared to mineral diesel, biodiesel reduces CO2 emissions by around 50–80 percent [13]. Fig. 14 depicts the effect of combining CQDs in B20 on CO2 emissions. In addition, CO2 emission yield percent of all fuel sample compared to D100 are displayed in Table 8 (the arrows showed increase or decrease emission of CO2 in different fuels). As shown in the graph and Table 6 the biodiesel emits 6.21 % lower CO2 compared to mineral diesel. In addition, by adding CQDs in B20 the CO2 emission increased slightly (CO2 emission was decreased in fuel sample B20+ CQDs100 ppm 34.44 % increased at speed of 2100 rpm, and B20+ CQDs300 ppm 33.54 % increased at speed of 1800 rpm). The B20 + CQDs 600 ppm sample had the highest CO2 emissions at speed of 1800 rpm 40.38 % compared with D100 and had 49.96 % compared with B20 owing to the complete combustion of fuel in the engine. CO is an odorless, tasteless and colorless gas that is the most polluting agent in the atmosphere [11]. Carbon monoxide (CO) emissions are generated fundamentally because of the imperfect combustion [13]. Fuel combination, fuel injection pressure, spray penetration, injection timing, and air/fuel ratio affect imperfect combustion. The CO emission of fuel mixtures with nanoparticles was shown in Fig. 15 . Besides, CO emission reduced yield percent of all fuel sample compared to D100 showed in Table 7
. The results of adding the 100 and 300 ppm to B20 were same as each other approximately (between 5.55 and 11.25 % can reduced CO emission compared to D100). However, the carbon monoxide emission were yield of B20+ C600 ppm was 33.85 % at speed of 1800 rpm compared with D100, which can reduce CO at the highest level. Comparison of CO and CO2, showing the balance behavior of CO and CO2 is inverse. Overall, Oxygenated additives play a crucial role in enhancing combustion efficiency by increasing the air-fuel ratio through the provision of additional oxygen, consequently lowering CO emissions [76]. Studies showed that nanoparticles can enhance fuel combustion efficiency by 11.7 % and 13.4 %, while simultaneously reducing air pollutants such as carbon monoxide by 24.2 % and 24.8 %, and unburned hydrocarbons by 11.5 % and 25.3 %, respectively [77,78].

NOx is the term commonly used to refer to the combination of nitrogen dioxide (NO2) and nitric oxide (NO) present in exhaust emissions. Generally, the high temperature of the combustion process is the main cause of nitrogen oxide production in engines. When the temperature in the combustion process rises to a certain level, O2 molecules break down to form O atoms, which generate nitrogen oxide in the absence of nitrogen. As a result, nitrogen oxide emissions can be decreased by reduction of local temperature combustion chamber [6,67]. Owing to, the more oxygen in biodiesel, it emits more NOx than diesel [15,76,and20]]. The formation NOx reduced when the biodiesel mixture combined with carbon nanoparticles. Nanoparticles heat sinks in the combustion chamber by decreasing temperatures and preventing hot spots, reduce production NOx. Furthermore, the NOx emissions were significantly diminished when nanoparticle was added to B20 compared to both B20 blended and diesel fuels [13]. The results also indicated that adding nanoparticles of CQDs reduces NOx emissions were reduced because of heat absorption. The nanoparticle acts as a factor, causing accelerated evaporation, breaking down NOx emission particles into O2 and N2, and resulting in lower NOx emissions. NOx emissions were reduced by increased catalytic activity and thermal conductivity, as well as improved fuel-air mixing disturbance. The combination of activating higher carbon combustion with the rate of oxidation of hydrocarbons reduces NOx emissions. The reduction in fuel burnt during the pre-mixed burning process resulted in a lower maximum combustion temperature and lower NOx emissions. The thermal impact of the nano-additive allows for the interaction of nitrogen and oxygen atoms at high combustion temperatures, thus emitting the least amount of NOx. To create excellent products with the fewest hydrocarbons and lowest NOx emissions, carbon nanomaterials increased heat transport, improved atomization of fuel droplets, and accelerated combustion [79]. As Fig. 16 and results yields of NOx emissions in all fuel samples in Table 8 show the added carbon dots nanoparticles (300 and 600 ppm) were the optimum samples for reduction of NOx which can reduce the NOx 26 % at the speed of 1800 rpm compared with diesel.

Unburned hydrocarbon emissions were known as a predominant pollutant from the engine. These emissions originate from insufficient combustion and enter the atmosphere together with some combustion reaction product. High quantity of UHC emission degrade engine performance, which might be mitigated by the addition of oxygen-based materials. Furthermore, lowering engine temperature has a similar effect on exhaust gas emissions. Higher level of UHC and CO engine's exhaust, results in lower engine efficiency. The reason why the nanoparticle mixture has a lower UHC emission is due to the accessibility of higher oxygen content and higher activation energy dissipated by the nanoparticles [11]. This compound helps burn the carbon deposit prevalent in the cylinder and helps remove residual nonionics at the cylinder-piston interface, ultimately reducing fuel quenching while reducing fuel losses, boosting combustion efficiency. As a result, lower carbon residue combustion activation temperature promotes combustion, which leads to lower UHC formation [80]. In this study, B20 reduced UHC emissions 13.28 %. This could be owing to the structural oxygen of biodiesel improving the oxidation of UHCs. The same result was observer in other experiments. Adding carbon dot nanoparticles to a diesel-biodiesel fuel mixture decreases the release of UHC. As Fig. 17 , and estimated reduced emission yield percent of UHC in different engine speeds in Table 9 show the added carbon dots nanoparticles (600 ppm) was the optimum sample for reduction of UHC, which can reduce the UHC 34 % at the rate of 2400 rpm compared with diesel. Otherwise, B20 + CQDs 100 and 300 ppm at the best performances displayed 6.20 %, and 11.25 % compared to D100 at the engine speeds of 2400 and 1800 rpm, respectively. It can be concluded that, increase concentration of CQDs in biodiesel-diesel blends, may lead to the higher combustion efficiency and lower UHC pollutant.

In this work, the pomegranate peel carbon quantum dots were prepared and used as fuel additive and biodiesel antioxidant. The engine test results demonstrated that, in comparison to D100 fuel, the optimal concentration of carbon quantum dots (600 ppm) resulted in notable enhancements in torque, power, CO, UHC, and NOx emissions, with improvements of 12.113 %, 18.606 %, 14.12 %, 33.85 %, and 26 %, respectively. This material possesses distinctive characteristics, including a small particle size ranging from 3 to 5 nm and a high surface area, which enhances its dispersion within the fuel, leading to more complete combustion. Consequently, the performance of the engine has been enhanced, resulting in a reduction of pollutant emissions. Antioxidant activity of pomegranate peel carbon quantum dots was examined by Rancimate test which can improved stability of biodiesel from 6 h to 92.21 h which was 7.5 time better than BHA (a petrochemical synthetic antioxidant). Due to composition and structure of carbon quantum dots, pomegranate peel carbon quantum dots was indicated a robust antioxidant activity. Surface functional groups like -OH, -COOH, and -NH2 play a key role in scavenging reactive nitrogen species by stabilizing electronic charge. In addition, the formation of radical adducts at sp2 carbon sites, where sp2-rich domains facilitate the interaction with radical species. Therefore, these promising results suggested pomegranate peel carbon quantum dots as a robust biodiesel antioxidant, and alternative fuel additive for improvement of CI engine operation and exhaust emission.
Sama Torkzaban: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Mostafa Feyzi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Leila Norouzi: Investigation.
